source: Article 15 (2019) — full screenplay; weighted to the 'harijan/bahujan/jan ginti' monologue, the 'aukaat' exchange, and the investigative-procedural frame
love_type: both — strong mainstream reach for a message-film, AND cult among socially-conscious viewers who quote its caste-critique lines as discourse ammunition
core_emotion: moral outrage crystallizing into resolve — the discomfort of privilege confronting injustice and choosing to act

dialogue_style.register: two registers in tension — the privileged officer's clean urban Hindi/English vs the ground-level dialect of caste hierarchy; argument-driven, idea-dense
dialogue_style.rhythm: procedural Q&A that keeps surfacing systemic truths; charged set-piece monologues that detach as quotable theses
dialogue_style.stylization: grounded realism with deliberately literary, slogan-grade speeches
dialogue_style.why_it's_theirs: the film makes caste legible through precise, citable language — wordplay that turns sociology into something you can say

signature_dialogues.line: ...hum kabhi harijan ho jaate hain, kabhi bahujan. Bas 'jan' nahi rah paate ki 'Jan-Gan-Man' mein hamari bhi ginti ho sakey.
signature_dialogues.construction: triple wordplay on -jan (harijan/bahujan/jan) climaxing on the national anthem ('Jan-Gan-Man') — rhetorical escalation that lands on belonging
signature_dialogues.the_setup: a Dalit character answers the privileged claim that '70 years have changed everything'
signature_dialogues.presentation: spoken quietly, almost wearily, by the marginalized character rather than shouted — the calm delivery and the anthem reference make it dignified protest, not a rant; the wordplay rewards the listener with a click of recognition
signature_dialogues.payoff: reframes decades of labels as a denial of basic citizenship in a single linguistic turn
signature_dialogues.why_quotable: the -jan/Jan-Gan-Man pun is the film's thesis and travels as a standalone citation in caste debates

signature_dialogues.line: Aur aukaat kya hai? Jo hum dete hain wohi aukaat hai... Aukaat toh sabki...
signature_dialogues.construction: Socratic needling — defines 'status/worth' (aukaat) as something arbitrarily assigned by the powerful, exposing it as a construct
signature_dialogues.the_setup: casual conversation reveals the machinery by which caste 'keeps people in their place'
signature_dialogues.presentation: delivered matter-of-factly by perpetrators, which is what chills — bigotry stated as banal common sense, no villain music
signature_dialogues.payoff: lets the audience see oppression as ordinary administration rather than cartoon evil
signature_dialogues.why_quotable: the interrogation of 'aukaat' is reused to call out everyday casteism/classism

the_peaks.moment: the 'harijan/bahujan/jan' monologue answering '70 years changed everything'
the_peaks.iconic_line: Bas 'jan' nahi rah paate ki 'Jan-Gan-Man' mein hamari bhi ginti ho sakey.
the_peaks.why_loved: names a vast injustice with a single elegant pun — intellectually thrilling and emotionally damning

the_peaks.moment: the officer wading into the sewage/marsh to confront what he'd rather not see
the_peaks.why_loved: privilege physically descending into the reality it ignored — visceral moral image

wish_fulfillment: an honest, powerful insider who refuses to look away and actually uses his position to force justice — the protector the powerless rarely get
character_magnetism: Ayan, the privileged outsider-officer whose dawning conscience the audience rides; Nishad and Gaura's grounded dignity
audacity: a mainstream star-vehicle naming caste atrocity bluntly, using the national anthem itself as an indictment — politically risky directness
portability: the -jan wordplay and the 'aukaat' interrogation circulate as quotable anti-caste rhetoric
why_cult_not_just_good: activist and student audiences cite its lines as rhetorical tools in real arguments — the script became discourse
transferable_love_triggers: Build a thesis-line on layered wordplay that climaxes on a shared cultural touchstone (the anthem) — the pun gives a click of recognition that makes outrage quotable.
Have the marginalized character state the hardest truth quietly and wearily, not shouting — dignity out-persuades a rant.
Let bigotry be spoken as banal common sense by ordinary people — ordinary evil is more chilling and more honest than a cartoon villain.
Give the audience a privileged surrogate who descends (literally) into the reality they ignore, so discomfort becomes a journey, not a lecture.
Turn an abstract concept ('aukaat'/status) into a thing a character interrogates out loud, so the audience inherits a tool to question it in real life.
confidence: high — both signature passages confirmed verbatim in script
